investing.title: Stock Market Investing
investing.description: Learn to build and manage a high-performing investment portfolio. From basics to advanced wealth management strategies.

modules.title: Introduction to Financial Markets
modules.description: Understand how stock markets work, their history, and their role in the economy.

content.title: What is the Stock Market?
content.body:
The stock market is an organized marketplace where financial securities (stocks, bonds, etc.) are traded. It allows companies to raise capital and investors to grow their wealth. Major stock exchanges include the NYSE (New York Stock Exchange), NASDAQ, Euronext Paris (CAC 40), London Stock Exchange (FTSE 100), and Tokyo Stock Exchange (Nikkei 225).

A stock's price is determined by supply and demand: if more people want to buy than sell, the price goes up, and vice versa. This mechanism reflects investors' expectations about a company's future financial health.

content.title: Key Market Participants
content.body:
**Institutional investors**: Pension funds, insurance companies, sovereign wealth funds. They account for about 70% of trading volume.

**Retail investors**: Individuals investing their personal savings through brokerage accounts.

**Market makers**: They ensure market liquidity by continuously offering buy and sell prices.

**Regulators**: The SEC in the US, FCA in the UK, AMF in France. They oversee market integrity and transparency.

content.title: Why Invest in the Stock Market?
content.body:
Historically, the stock market offers the best long-term returns. The S&P 500 has delivered an annualized return of approximately 10% over the last 100 years (before inflation).

**Advantages:**
- Protection against inflation
- Compound interest effect
- Liquidity (ability to sell quickly)
- Accessible diversification
- Passive income (dividends)

**Risks:**
- Short-term volatility
- Risk of capital loss
- Currency risk (international investments)
- Liquidity risk on small caps

keyPoints: The stock market is an organized marketplace for trading securities
Prices are determined by supply and demand
Institutional investors dominate trading volume
Historical average return is approximately 10% per year
Investing carries risks of capital loss

exercises.question: Explain in your own words the difference between the primary and secondary market. Give a concrete example for each.
exercises.correction:
**Primary market**: Where new securities are issued. When a company goes public (IPO) or issues new shares. Example: Facebook's IPO in 2012 at $38 per share.

**Secondary market**: Where previously issued securities are traded between investors. This is what we commonly call 'the stock market'. Example: Buying Apple shares today on NASDAQ.

exercises.question: An investor buys 100 shares at $50 each. Six months later, the share is worth $58 and they received $1.50 in dividends per share. Calculate the total return.
exercises.correction:
**Total return calculation:**
- Initial investment: 100 × $50 = $5,000
- Capital gain: 100 × ($58 - $50) = $800
- Dividends: 100 × $1.50 = $150
- Total gain: $800 + $150 = $950
- Total return: $950 / $5,000 = 19%
- Annualized return: (1 + 0.19)^(12/6) - 1 ≈ 41.6%

quiz.question: What is the primary role of the stock market?
options: To facilitate securities trading and corporate financing

quiz.question: What historical annualized return has the S&P 500 delivered over 100 years?
Who represents approximately 70% of trading volume?

modules.title: Asset Classes Overview
modules.description: Stocks, bonds, commodities, REITs, cryptocurrencies: understand each asset class in depth.

content.title: Stocks (Equities)
content.body:
A stock represents ownership in a company. As a shareholder, you're entitled to:
- Dividends (share of distributed profits)
- Voting rights at general meetings
- Capital gains if the price rises

**Types of stocks:**
- **Growth stocks**: High-growth companies reinvesting profits (e.g., Amazon, Tesla). High valuation, little or no dividends.
- **Value/Income stocks**: Mature companies paying regular dividends (e.g., Coca-Cola, Johnson & Johnson). More reasonable valuation.
- **Defensive stocks**: Sectors resistant to crises (healthcare, food, utilities). Less volatile.
- **Cyclical stocks**: Performance tied to economic cycles (automotive, luxury, banks).
- **Blue Chips**: Large-cap, sector leaders, considered the safest.

content.title: Bonds (Fixed Income)
content.body:
A bond is a debt instrument. You lend money to an issuer (government, corporation) who repays you with interest.

**Key characteristics:**
- **Face value**: Amount borrowed (often $1,000)
- **Coupon**: Interest paid periodically
- **Maturity**: Repayment date
- **Rating**: Credit rating (AAA = very safe, C = very risky)

**Types of bonds:**
- **Government bonds (Treasuries in US, Gilts in UK)**: Safest, lower yield
- **Investment Grade corporate bonds**: Moderate risk, intermediate yield
- **High Yield (junk bonds)**: High risk, high yield
- **Convertible bonds**: Can be converted to shares
- **Inflation-linked bonds (TIPS)**: Protection against inflation

content.title: Commodities, Real Estate & Alternatives
content.body:
**Commodities:**
- Gold: Safe haven during uncertainty
- Oil: Tied to economic cycles
- Industrial metals: Copper, lithium (energy transition)
- Agricultural products: Wheat, corn, coffee

Accessible through ETFs, futures, or producer stocks.

**REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts):**
- Companies holding real estate portfolios
- Required to distribute a large portion of income
- Sectors: offices, retail, logistics, healthcare
- Examples: Prologis, Realty Income

**Alternative assets:**
- Cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum)
- Private equity
- Infrastructure
- Art, wine, collectibles

keyPoints: Stocks offer the best long-term returns but are volatile
Bonds provide stability and regular income
Diversification across asset classes reduces overall risk
Each class has its own risk/return characteristics
Alternative assets complement a diversified portfolio

exercises.question: Rank the following asset classes from least to most risky: Emerging market stocks, AAA Government bonds, Gold, Blue Chip stocks, High Yield bonds, Cryptocurrencies. Justify your ranking.
exercises.correction:
**From least to most risky:**
1. AAA Government bonds - Minimal risk, government guarantee
2. Gold - Safe haven, moderate volatility
3. High Yield bonds - Higher default risk but still bonds
4. Blue Chip stocks - Stock volatility but solid companies
5. Emerging market stocks - Geopolitical + currency + volatility risk
6. Cryptocurrencies - Extreme volatility, no intrinsic value

This ranking is approximate. Actual risk depends on investment horizon and market conditions.

quiz.question: What is the fundamental difference between a stock and a bond?
options: A stock is ownership, a bond is debt

quiz.question: Defensive stocks typically belong to which sector?

modules.title: Building Your Portfolio
modules.description: Diversification principles, asset allocation, and building a portfolio tailored to your profile.

content.title: Your Investor Profile
content.body:
Before investing, you must determine your profile by assessing:

**1. Your investment horizon:**
- Short term (< 2 years): favor bonds and money market funds
- Medium term (2-8 years): stock/bond mix
- Long term (> 8 years): stock-dominant

**2. Your risk tolerance:**
- Conservative: accepts 5-10% volatility
- Balanced: accepts 10-20% volatility
- Dynamic: accepts > 20% volatility

**3. Your goals:**
- Capital growth
- Regular income
- Retirement planning
- Specific project

content.title: Diversification: Your Best Protection
content.body:
Harry Markowitz, Nobel Prize in Economics, demonstrated that diversification is 'the only free lunch in finance'.

**Multi-level diversification:**
1. **By asset class**: Stocks + Bonds + Real Estate + Commodities
2. **Geographic**: Europe + US + Asia + Emerging
3. **Sectoral**: Tech + Healthcare + Finance + Energy + Consumer
4. **By size**: Large caps + Mid caps + Small caps
5. **By style**: Growth + Value + Blend

**Correlation:**
The ideal is combining uncorrelated assets. When stocks fall, bonds often rise, creating a buffer effect.

content.title: Classic Allocation Models
content.body:
**Conservative Portfolio:**
- 30% Stocks (Blue Chips, dividends)
- 50% Bonds (Government + Investment Grade)
- 10% REITs
- 10% Gold / Cash

**Balanced Portfolio:**
- 50% Stocks (geographically diversified)
- 30% Bonds
- 10% REITs
- 10% Alternatives

**Dynamic Portfolio:**
- 70% Stocks (includes Growth and Small Caps)
- 15% High Yield Bonds
- 10% Commodities
- 5% Cryptocurrencies

**Aggressive Portfolio (Risk Taker):**
- 85% Stocks (Growth, Small Caps, Emerging)
- 10% Cryptocurrencies
- 5% Options / Derivatives

**Simple age-based rule:**
Bond % = Your age (e.g., 30 years old → 30% bonds, 70% stocks)

keyPoints: Define your profile before investing (horizon, risk, goals)
Diversification reduces risk without reducing expected return
Correlation between assets is as important as the assets themselves
Adapt your allocation to your age and situation
There is no universal perfect portfolio

exercises.question: Marie, 35 years old, stable job, wants to prepare for retirement in 30 years. She has $50,000 to invest and doesn't like seeing her portfolio lose more than 15%. Propose a justified asset allocation.
exercises.correction:
**Marie's profile:**
- Horizon: Long term (30 years) → favorable for stocks
- Tolerance: Moderate (max -15%) → balanced profile
- Objective: Retirement → capital growth

**Proposed allocation:**
- 55% Diversified stocks ($27,500)
  - 30% World ETF (MSCI World)
  - 15% US ETF (S&P 500)
  - 10% Emerging Markets ETF
- 25% Bonds ($12,500)
  - 15% Government bonds
  - 10% Investment Grade corporate
- 10% REITs ($5,000)
- 10% Gold ETF ($5,000)

This allocation limits maximum historical loss to approximately 15-20% while offering an expected return of 6-7% per year over 30 years.

quiz.question: According to the simplified rule, what bond allocation for a 40-year-old?
Which Nobel Prize winner theorized portfolio diversification?

modules.title: Active vs Passive vs Semi-Active Management
modules.description: Compare management styles, their pros and cons, and choose the one that suits you.

content.title: Passive (Index) Management
content.body:
Passive management consists of replicating a stock index without trying to beat it.

**Principle:** Buy an ETF that faithfully reproduces the performance of an index like the S&P 500 or MSCI World.

**Advantages:**
- Very low fees (0.05% to 0.40% per year vs 1.5-2% for active)
- Outperforms 85-90% of active managers over 10-20 years
- Simplicity: no need to analyze companies
- Immediate diversification
- Optimized taxation (fewer transactions)

**Disadvantages:**
- No protection in bear markets
- Performance equals the market, never higher
- Concentration risk in cap-weighted indices

**Famous advocates:** Warren Buffett recommends retail investors buy an S&P 500 ETF. John Bogle (Vanguard founder) popularized this approach.

content.title: Active Management
content.body:
Active management aims to beat the market through stock selection or market timing.

**Approaches:**
- **Stock picking**: Selecting individual stocks
- **Market timing**: Anticipating market movements
- **Tactical allocation**: Overweighting/underweighting sectors

**Advantages:**
- Potential for outperformance
- Possible protection in bear markets
- Ability to avoid controversial companies

**Disadvantages:**
- High fees eroding performance
- 85-90% of active managers underperform their index long-term
- Requires time, skill, and discipline
- Psychological biases (overconfidence, loss aversion)

**When active management has an edge:**
- Inefficient markets (small caps, emerging)
- Crisis periods (ability to go to cash)
- Specialized niches

content.title: Semi-Active Management (Smart Beta / Factor Investing)
content.body:
Semi-active management combines advantages of both approaches with systematic rules.

**Academically proven performance factors:**
- **Value**: Buying undervalued stocks (low P/E, low P/B)
- **Momentum**: Following price trends
- **Quality**: Profitable companies with solid balance sheets
- **Size**: Overweighting small caps
- **Low Volatility**: Less volatile stocks outperform risk-adjusted
- **Dividends**: Growing dividend stocks

**The Core-Satellite approach:**
Popular strategy combining:
- **Core (70-80%)**: Broad index ETFs (passive)
- **Satellites (20-30%)**: Targeted active positions (stock picks, sector ETFs, thematic)

This provides a solid, low-cost base while allowing alpha-seeking on a controlled portion.

keyPoints: Passive management beats 85-90% of active managers long-term
Fees are the most predictive factor of future performance
Smart Beta offers an interesting compromise
Core-Satellite combines the best of both worlds
No universal answer: choose based on your time and skills

exercises.question: Pierre invests $10,000 in an active fund with 1.8% annual fees. Sophie invests the same in an ETF at 0.20% fees. If both achieve 8% gross annual return for 25 years, calculate the final difference.
exercises.correction:
**Pierre (active, 1.8% fees):**
Net return: 8% - 1.8% = 6.2%/year
Final capital: $10,000 × (1.062)^25 = $45,381

**Sophie (ETF, 0.20% fees):**
Net return: 8% - 0.20% = 7.8%/year
Final capital: $10,000 × (1.078)^25 = $65,573

**Difference: $20,192** or +44.5% in Sophie's favor!

This shows the massive impact of fees through compounding. Over 25 years, 1.6% extra annual fees cost over $20,000 on a $10,000 investment.

quiz.question: What percentage of active managers underperform their index long-term?
Which strategy combines a passive core with active satellites?

modules.title: ETFs In-Depth
modules.description: Everything about ETFs: types, selection, fees, replication, taxation, and usage strategies.

content.title: ETF Anatomy
content.body: An ETF (Exchange Traded Fund) is a listed fund that replicates an index. Key features include continuous trading like stocks, very low management fees (TER), full transparency, and high liquidity. Replication methods include Physical (full), Optimized sampling, and Synthetic (swap-based).

keyPoints: TER is the most important fee metric
Prefer physical replication ETFs
AUM should exceed $100M
DCA on a World ETF is the simplest effective strategy

quiz.question: What is considered an 'excellent' TER?

modules.title: Fundamental Analysis & Valuation
modules.description: Learn to evaluate a company: financial ratios, balance sheet reading, valuation methods.

content.title: Key Ratios
content.body: P/E Ratio, P/B, PEG, EV/EBITDA, Dividend Yield — understand each fundamental metric and how to use them for stock analysis.

keyPoints: P/E alone isn't enough: cross multiple ratios
ROE and ROIC measure value creation
DCF is the most rigorous method
Always compare with same-sector companies

quiz.question: A P/E of 8x generally suggests:

modules.title: Macroeconomic Analysis
modules.description: Impact of interest rates, inflation, economic cycles, and monetary policy on your investments.

content.title: Economic Cycles
content.body: The economy evolves in 4 phases: Expansion, Peak, Contraction, and Trough. Each phase impacts different asset classes and sectors differently.

keyPoints: Economic cycles dictate relative asset class performance
Interest rates are the most important macro factor
Yield curve inversion signals recession
Adapt sector allocation to cycle phase

quiz.question: A PMI of 52 indicates:

modules.title: Investor Psychology & Discipline
modules.description: Master cognitive biases, emotional management, and the discipline needed to perform.

content.title: Cognitive Biases in Investing
content.body: Daniel Kahneman (Nobel Prize) demonstrated our brains aren't wired for rational investing. Key biases include loss aversion, confirmation bias, FOMO, and herd mentality.

keyPoints: Cognitive biases are your worst enemy
Loss aversion leads to irrational decisions
A written plan and automation are your best tools
Discipline beats intelligence in investing

quiz.question: Loss aversion bias means:
options: A loss weighs ~2x more psychologically than an equal gain

trading.title: Trading & Technical Analysis
trading.description: Master technical analysis, chart patterns, indicators, and professional trading strategies.

modules.title: Trading Fundamentals
modules.description: Trading basics: vocabulary, order types, timeframes, and first technical analysis concepts.

content.title: Trading vs Investing
content.body: Trading and investing differ fundamentally. Trading focuses on short-term price fluctuations using technical analysis, while investing aims for long-term capital growth using fundamental analysis.

keyPoints: Trading seeks to profit from short-term fluctuations
Stop loss is MANDATORY for every position
Always analyze at least 2-3 timeframes
Never trade against the higher timeframe trend

quiz.question: A 5% trailing stop on a $100 stock that rises to $120 triggers at:

modules.title: Japanese Candlesticks
modules.description: Master candlestick reading: anatomy, reversal and continuation patterns.

content.title: Candlestick Anatomy
content.body: Each candle represents 4 prices: Open, High, Low, Close. Green/white body = bullish, Red/black body = bearish. Wicks reveal price rejection zones.

keyPoints: Wicks reveal price rejection zones
Reversal patterns are more reliable near support/resistance
Always wait for next-candle confirmation
Higher timeframes give more reliable signals

quiz.question: A Hammer at the top of an uptrend is called:

modules.title: Support, Resistance & Trends
modules.description: Technical analysis foundations: identify key levels and market trends.

content.title: Support & Resistance
content.body: Support is a price level where demand stops the decline. Resistance is where supply stops the rise. A broken support becomes resistance and vice versa (polarity).

keyPoints: Supports and resistances are ZONES, not lines
Broken support becomes resistance (and vice versa)
The trend is your friend
Volume confirms breakouts

quiz.question: A broken support becomes:

modules.title: Essential Technical Indicators
modules.description: RSI, MACD, Moving Averages, Bollinger Bands, Volume: master the key indicators.

content.title: Moving Averages
content.body: Moving averages smooth price to identify trends. Key ones: EMA 9/20 for short-term, SMA 50 for medium-term, SMA 200 for long-term. Golden Cross and Death Cross are major signals.

keyPoints: SMA 200 is the long-term trend judge
RSI and MACD divergences are the most reliable signals
Bollinger Squeeze announces explosive moves
Maximum 2-3 indicators on your charts

quiz.question: A Golden Cross is the crossing of:

modules.title: Chart Patterns & Figures
modules.description: Triangles, double tops, head and shoulders, flags: identify and trade chart patterns.

content.title: Reversal Patterns
content.body: Head and Shoulders is the most reliable reversal pattern (~80%). Double Top/Bottom and Triple formations are also key reversal signals to master.

keyPoints: Head and Shoulders is ~80% reliable
Triangles break in trend direction 75% of the time
Fibonacci golden zone (50-61.8%) is most watched
Always confirm patterns with volume

quiz.question: The Fibonacci Golden Ratio is at:

modules.title: Advanced Price Action
modules.description: Pure Price Action trading: market structure, liquidity zones, order flow, and smart money concepts.

content.title: Market Structure
content.body: Price Action analyzes raw price without indicators. Key concepts include Break of Structure (BOS), Change of Character (CHoCH), Order Blocks, and Smart Money Concepts.

keyPoints: Markets are driven by institutional liquidity needs
CHoCH is the first sign of trend change
Order Blocks are institutional decision zones
Always trade HTF direction with LTF entry

quiz.question: A CHoCH indicates:

modules.title: Professional Risk Management
modules.description: Professional risk management techniques, position sizing, and capital protection.

content.title: Golden Rules of Risk Management
content.body: Never risk more than 1-2% per trade. Always use stop losses. Minimum 1:2 risk/reward ratio. Set daily and weekly loss limits. Position sizing is the most important skill in trading.

keyPoints: NEVER risk more than 1-2% per trade
Position sizing is the most important trading skill
1:2 R:R is profitable with just 40% win rate
Trading journal is mandatory for improvement

quiz.question: With 1% risk per trade, how many consecutive losses to lose 10%?
